title: ParaDG: Disagreement-Gated Paraphrase Blending for Robust Text-to-Image Diffusion
description: ParaDG is a training-free, robustness-oriented extension of AAPB-style multi-prompt blending for Stable Diffusion v1.5. It uses only audited paraphrases of the same prompt and increases blending strength only when those equivalent prompts disagree during denoising.
motivation: The novelty gap is narrow but still meaningful. Existing work already covers strong single-prompt guidance, general multi-prompt blending, and robustness evaluation with equivalent prompts, but it does not directly test whether equivalent-prompt disagreement can serve as a specialized inference-time signal for wording robustness under a small, reproducible setup.
proposed_approach: Use Stable Diffusion v1.5 with 30 DDIM steps, seeds [11, 22, 33], and 8 sparse guided steps. Restrict the study to prompts that fit one pre-registered slot schema with up to two objects, bound attributes, counts, and an optional directed relation. For each prompt, create up to two controlled paraphrases from a fixed rewrite template set and accept them only if they preserve every slot exactly under a checklist audit. At guided timesteps, compute a paraphrase consensus prediction and apply a timestep-adaptive blend toward that consensus, multiplied by a simple disagreement gate derived from variance across paraphrase-conditioned noise predictions. Compare ParaDG against vanilla SD1.5, static paraphrase consensus, adaptive paraphrase blending without gating, faithful AAPB, and one single-prompt baseline on a 96-prompt matrix scoped to the 8-hour budget.

related_work.title: NeuroPrompts: An Adaptive Framework to Optimize Prompts for Text-to-Image Generation
related_work.authors: Shachar Rosenman, Vasudev Lal, Phillip Howard
related_work.how_it_relates: Represents prompt optimization methods that search for stronger wording rather than making equivalent wording agree.

hypothesis: For prompts with at least one audited paraphrase, disagreement-gated paraphrase blending will improve compositional faithfulness and paraphrase robustness over vanilla SD1.5, static paraphrase consensus, and adaptive paraphrase blending without gating; on the overlap subset it will also outperform or match a faithful AAPB reproduction on robustness-specific metrics without unacceptable diversity collapse.

confirm: ParaDG beats vanilla SD1.5 and both ungated paraphrase baselines on at least two of the three main held-out compositional categories.
ParaDG improves Paraphrase Robustness Score on the overlap subset over vanilla SD1.5, static consensus, and adaptive paraphrase blending without gating.
ParaDG beats faithful AAPB on PRS, or matches AAPB on PRS while exceeding it on at least one compositional category.
Human adjudication on the stratified subset does not overturn the robustness conclusion.
Any robustness gain is not accompanied by more than a 20 percent LPIPS drop relative to vanilla without matching semantic improvement.
refute: ParaDG fails to beat the adaptive ungated paraphrase baseline on robustness or faithfulness metrics.
Apparent robustness gains disappear under human adjudication or depend on invalid paraphrases.
Approved paraphrase coverage is too low in numeracy or relation categories to support the core claim.
The main effect can be explained by generic adaptive paraphrase blending rather than disagreement gating.
